name: Tobi Lütke
title: Tobi Lütke Wanted to Sell Snowboards. He Built an Empire Instead.
dek: The German-born coder who couldn't find software to run his snowboard shop, so he wrote it himself — and accidentally armed a generation of online sellers.
body_html:
Some founders chase a giant vision from day one. Tobi Lütke just wanted to sell snowboards on the internet without losing his mind. The empire was a happy accident.

The Snowboard Shop That Wasn't

In the early 2000s, Lütke — a programmer who'd trained as a coder in Germany before landing in Canada — set out to open an online snowboard store called Snowdevil. There was just one problem: the e-commerce software available at the time was, to put it generously, terrible. So Lütke did what builders do. He stopped trying to find a tool and started writing one.

The store was fine. The software, though? The software was the gold mine. He realized he wasn't actually in the snowboard business — he was sitting on the thing every other would-be online seller desperately needed. Shopify was born from that pivot.

Empowering the Little Guy as a Whole Philosophy

Here's the creative heart of it: Lütke built Shopify around a near-religious belief in lowering the barrier to entrepreneurship. The product's genius was making it absurdly simple for anyone — a candle-maker, a t-shirt hustler, your cousin with the crochet side gig — to spin up a real, professional storefront without writing a line of code.

Shopify's mission wasn't to build stores. It was to make more entrepreneurs exist.

The brand world reflected that ethos: approachable, builder-friendly, obsessed with the underdog merchant rather than the corporate giant. Lütke cultivated a beloved app ecosystem, themes that let stores express real personality, and tools that grew alongside a business from first sale to scale. He also became something of a developer-philosopher online, openly geeking out about craft, systems, and the joy of building — the rare CEO whose engineering nerd credibility was the marketing.

From Side Project to Commerce Backbone

The growth was staggering. Shopify rode the explosion of the creator economy and the online-shopping boom to become one of the most valuable tech companies to come out of Canada, powering millions of merchants worldwide and processing an enormous volume of sales. The company went public and turned Lütke into one of his generation's standout tech billionaires — though he's stayed famously focused on craft over flash.

What makes him a Millennials Creatives icon is the inversion at the core of his story. He didn't set out to disrupt retail or dethrone anyone. He just solved his own annoying problem so well that millions of other people quietly built their dreams on top of his solution.

Tobi Lütke went looking for software to sell snowboards. He found a way to help the whole world sell everything else.
